sections.subject: History

entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Canadian History: Grades 5-6, World History: Grades 4-6, Ancient History: Grades 5-6

About World History: Grades 4-6
Through the reading of history stories and biographies, students experience what life was like in different times and places. They are exposed to the everyday experiences as well as the major events from our current history cycle (1800-1900). Students will also view images, reference maps, and work on their century charts and Book of Centuries as part of their lessons.

About Ancient History: Grades 5-6
Study history stories about the ancient civilizations that lived from 100 BC to 350 AD. View images and reference atlases and maps to set the people and events in their time and place. Update History Charts and Book of Centuries as appropriate for student level.

About Canadian History: Grades 5-6
Read about Canadian history from 1800-1900 through a variety of texts: engaging narratives, history spines, primary source documents, and historical fiction. These books, which offer a range of perspectives, will immerse students in the time period and allow them to see the world through the eyes of those who lived it. Students will view images and reference atlases and maps to set the people and events in their time and place. Update History Charts and Book of Centuries as appropriate for student level.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
World History: Grades 4-6
This course is the same for 4th-6th graders. In grade 5, students may begin their Book of Centuries.

Canadian History: Grades 5-6
This course is the same for students in grades 5 and 6. In grade 5, students may begin their Book of Centuries. It could be easily modified for students in grade 4, if combining.

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

History: Grades 5-6 (Canada)
☐ Read the document in the Quick Links about History Timelines and Charts.

☐ We encourage you to pick a biography for each term to read in the afternoons. The Bookshelf has many recommendations.

entries.title: Term Prep & Teacher Tips
entries.content:
Canadian History: Grades 5-6
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune is a historical novel that recounts the brave rescue of sailors from the HMS Tribune by a thirteen-year old boy known only as "Joe Cracker" to history. The author Jacqueline Halsey imagines a backstory for him, and parents may wish to pre-read the reveal of Joe's parentage on page 145-146 in advance to decide what is best for your particular student(s), especially those on the younger end of the age range. While the circumstances of Joe's birth, and the small boat he later uses in his rescue, both caused the fracturing of a family, Joe himself and the boat are what ultimately mend and heal the remaining family members. Some may choose to read the story aloud to edit on the fly if needed and to encourage discussion. Others will be fine with the content as is. We encourage all to provide an inviting space for students to ask questions and share thoughts as they process what they are reading in their books. There is always the option of substituting for Belle of Batoche from Grades 2-4 or Jeremy's War from Grades 7-8.

books.title: Commodore Perry in the Land of the Shogun
Redemption: The Church in Ancient Times
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

blocks.text:
• Read through the lesson and gather any materials or find any necessary links. 

• If you have not already read the book, you may want to read the selected pages in advance.

• Connect to previous lessons by asking students to recall where they left off. 

• If students have difficulty recalling, teachers might try showing them a picture from the reading, sharing something the teacher remembers, or prompting them with the first sentence of the last reading. This will often refresh their memory enough to allow them to recall.

• If students still cannot recall anything from the last reading, teachers might consider continuing anyway (with a shorter reading this time) or repeating the previous lesson, depending on the reading and the nature of the book.

• Use a picture, map, or engaging question to pique the student's interest in the day's lesson. 

• If necessary, you may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding.

• Read the day's passage out loud. When reading to pre-readers, point to the words as you read, even if it seems like they aren't paying attention.

• Move at the student’s pace for the lesson time rather than a particular number of pages. It is vitally important that teachers not try to ‘force feed’ a student more than they can consume in one sitting.

• Students who have a difficult time sitting still or listening may benefit from a fidget of their choice, such as a piece of putty or clay or an afghan; flexible positioning, such as a wobble seat or inverted sitting; or sensory modulation, such as a weighted vest or blanket or adjustment of light, temperature, or sound.

• Have students retell the passage orally.

• Alternatively, learners may use words, pictures, Legos, dolls, etc., to process and convey ideas in the manner most natural to them.  

• Teachers may take turns to model.

• Narration is a complex skill requiring the coordination of several parts of the brain. If a student struggles to narrate in general, with a specific subject or book, or on one day but not others, then this is an opportunity to learn. Observe when they struggle with this or any other activity today. Notice facial expressions and body language; listen to what they say about why it is hard or how they feel about it. Take note of these observations for later, tell them that you see how hard they are working, and then offer whatever support they need to end on a positive note. If necessary, take a break for the day.

• Give the student an opportunity to share thoughts on the lesson and any connections made.  

• To stimulate discussion, you could ask them what they thought about the passage or ask an open-ended question.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune, Canadian atlas or map of Nova Scotia

➜ INTRO
The years 1800-1900 were a momentous century for the history of Canada. It saw wars, rebellions, large waves of immigration, new and expanded settlements, exploration to the west and north, and Canada's Confederation of 1867, with more provinces and territories joining in the decades that followed. This course uses the narrative history The Story of Canada to provide an overview of the major events of the years 1800-1900, and a historical novel, a picture book, and a graphic novel to immerse students in three distinct moments in time: life in a fishing village on Nova Scotia's Chebucto Peninsula at the turn of the century, the life of Tecumseh, a Shawnee chief and British army ally in the War of 1812, and the Confederation Conferences of 1864, where the wheels of Canada's Confederacy were set in motion.
The first book, Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune, tells the story of a thirteen-year old boy who risked his life to save drowning sailors from the wreck of the H.M.S. Tribune, off the coast of Nova Scotia. This historical fiction novel imagines a backstory for the boy only known to history as "Joe Cracker," and it paints a detailed picture of what life was like in a small fishing village around 1800.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1-13 "Wisps of fog" - "all about the ocean."
• Who are the characters you meet as the novel opens? How are they related to each other? What are they like?
You may choose to keep a character list with some notes.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resources: Canadian Student Atlas p. or any other map (including online) showing Nova Scotia. 
Locate Halifax and the Chebucto Peninsula. Does your map show Herring Cove?

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Explore Herring Cove
Watch a video tour of Herring Cove with Senator Stan Kutcher. If you're short on time, feel free to skip 1:58-2:54. This whole video could be saved for another point in the course, but it touches on many themes of the novel, so it's definitely not a video to miss!
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Please read the header pages for this course which lists the page numbers for the sensitive content you may wish to preview.

lessons.title: 25m World History: Grades 4-6 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Under the Hawthorn Tree

➜ INTRO
1. When you think of Ireland, what comes to mind? Perhaps you thought of St. Patrick who went to Ireland to share the gospel. Ireland is often called the Emerald Isle because of its lush greenery. It is considered an enchanting place to visit.
				∞ Image Link: The Cliffs of Moher

1. However, in the 1840s tragedy struck Ireland that changed it forever. There was a terrible famine and many people either died or left the country to go to America. Today one-sixth of US citizens (43 million) identify their national background as Irish. How did this famine occur and what was it like to live in Ireland at this time? This book is a fictional account of an Irish family and what they did to survive. The author is from Ireland and has sequels to this book if you want to read more. 


➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.1- beginning of ch.2 p.1-11 "The air felt" - "they reached home."

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link:

lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from the opening chapters of the novel?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.13 "I broke my arm" - "Next job, Eli's shed."
• As you read, compare Joe's daily life to yours. For example, have you or anyone in your family ever broken a bone? How was it treated? How was Joe's treatment different? Or, what are your chores in the morning? Are they similar or different to Joe's?

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading about the types of work Joe has to do?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.48-60 "Ten long days of tides" - "to the old shed."
• Earlier in the novel, we learned that Joe has never been to school. This was not unusual two hundred years ago, when children were often needed to work inside or outside the home to help support their families. Why do you think Joe has never been to school? How does he learn what he needs to know?

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about Race Day from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.111-124 "Winter arrived that night" - "Frederick lagged behind."
• What has happened to the Tribune? Why won't the captain receive assistance from either other Navy ships or the local fishermen?

➜ VIEW
Look at this painting by maritime painter Thomas Whitcombe (1763-1824) of the Capture of the Tribune in 1796 by the British Navy. The Tribune was originally part of the French navy before its capture. What do you notice in the painting about these types of ships called frigates?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Today's reading is a bit longer than the others, so you may want to leave extra time, or finish the reading in the afternoon.

lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from the reading last week about the Tribune's predicament?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.125-136 "It was toasty warm" - "He'd never know."
• What do you predict is going to happen? Why do you think Gram told Joe his father's name in this moment?
• Remember to continue adding to your character list as you learn more about the characters.

➜ READ & DISCUSS
1. Read an account of the wreck of the Tribune from Nova Scotia's Maritime Museum of the Atlantic. 
∞ Article Link: HMS Tribune - 1797
2. Read a second account of the wreck, taken from contemporary sources. Numerous details from the novel appear in this account - can you identify some of them?
∞ Article Link: Tribune

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What details were most vivid from the reading last week?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.151-169 "The next weeks passed" - "come from my imagination."
• What are Joe's impressions of Halifax? While Halifax is only fifteen kilometres from Herring Cove (and about a 20 minute car trip), this would be a great distance to someone like Joe in 1797.
• Why did Joe decide to stay in Herring Cove, and why do you think he chose a pair of corduroy pants as a reward? Why do you think that detail stuck out so vividly to the author?
• What did you think of the novel?

➜ VIEW
Look at images of the monument to the HMS Tribune at to Joe Cracker.
Joe Cracker Joseph Shortt HMS Tribune

terms.content:
Canadian History: Grades 5-6
· Describe what life was like in Herring Cove, Nova Scotia around 1800; consider historical details such as food, shelter, clothing, transportation, medical care, family life, communication, etc.
